hook.text: Babylonians tracked the sky on clay tablets over 3,000 years ago. They could predict eclipses centuries in advance.

buildup.text: They divided the sky into twelve constellations along the ecliptic—the origin of the zodiac signs we know today.

discovery.text: Their records span centuries. This long baseline of data let them spot patterns invisible in a single lifetime.

twist.text: They didn't explain why events happened—just when. Their astronomy was predictive math without underlying theory.

climax.text: Greek astronomers inherited Babylonian data. Without those centuries of records, Greek theories couldn't have emerged.

punchline.text: Three thousand years of records on clay. Prediction before understanding.

quiz.question: What did Babylonian astronomers contribute to modern astronomy?
options: Centuries of sky observations, the zodiac, and eclipse prediction methods
The first telescope designs and lens-grinding techniques
The theory that Earth orbits the Sun
